title: Cedar wardrobe sachets
subtitle: Cedar shavings and cedarwood oil sachets to deter moths and freshen clothing storage
excerpt: Cedar contains natural oils that deter clothes moths and silverfish. Pack the shavings into muslin sachets and hang in wardrobes or tuck into drawers. Lightly sand the surface of the shavings when the scent fades to release fresh cedar oil.
sourceNotes: Cedar wood as a moth deterrent: Zhu et al., Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry (2001). Cedarwood oil (Juniperus virginiana) repellent properties: standard entomological reference.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale up to fill as many sachets as needed. Standard wardrobe advice is one sachet per 50 cm of hanging rail.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make sachets in batches and store the extras in a sealed bag until needed.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: The sachets last for two years or more. When the cedar smell fades, sand the shavings lightly with a piece of coarse sandpaper to expose fresh wood and release new oil, then add a few drops of cedarwood essential oil.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fine shavings of red cedar (Juniperus virginiana). The natural cedrol and thujopsene oils they contain have documented repellent activity against clothes moths and silverfish.

content.text: Cedarwood has been used to protect stored woolens and silks from moths for centuries.

release volatile oils that are repellent to clothes moths at the larval stage -- cedar does not kill moths already present, but deters them from laying eggs in a cedar-scented environment. A few drops of cedarwood essential oil boost the concentration and extend the working life of the sachet.

items.prepNote: about 0.75 ml; optional but extends life significantly

content.text: If using cedarwood essential oil, place the shavings in a bowl and scatter the drops evenly over the surface. Toss to distribute. Leave for five minutes to absorb.
Divide the shavings between four muslin squares, placing a generous handful (about 25 g) in the centre of each. Gather the fabric and tie firmly with ribbon.

Hang sachets inside wardrobes at the hanging rail level, or lay one flat in each drawer. Replace sachets that have lost their cedar smell -- or revive them by pressing the sachet firmly to release shavings, then lightly sanding the exposed surface with a small piece of coarse sandpaper. Add a few drops of cedarwood essential oil and reseal. One sachet per 50 cm of hanging rail is enough coverage.

Cedar stops moths from laying eggs and deters young larvae. It will not kill an active infestation. If you find larvae or holes in stored clothing, use a moth killer first and wash or freeze any affected items before relying on cedar sachets for protection.
